steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content:
Left side of the blank: an independent clause (“Relatively few of these particles are likely to reach Earth's surface”)
Right side of the blank: a dependent clause beginning with “since” (“since the meteoroids' compression of the air surrounding them causes the vast majority to burn up...”)

steps.title: Apply the rule for transition words within sentences
steps.content:
When a transition word like “however” is inserted within a sentence (not between two independent clauses), it must be surrounded by commas.

Choice B correctly places commas on both sides of “however,” to treat it as a parenthetical transition within the sentence.

The other choices violate the rule: A places a semicolon after “however,” C uses a semicolon as if connecting two independent clauses (but “since” introduces a dependent clause), and D breaks the sentence with a period, leaving only a fragment.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
